channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: The Lost Wild May Be the Dinosaur Horror Game You're Looking For
item.content:encoded:
You are part of the food chain in The Lost Wild. We had the chance to see a thirty minute demo, which was incredibly tense and showed the terrifying reality of what it would be like with dinosaurs around.

It was made clear early and stated several times that The Lost Wild is more of a tense cat and mouse game, not one in which you're going to be fighting dinosaurs. You're there to observe, adapt, and survive.

The tone is set early when in the demo we ran across some sort of researcher in a hazmat suit that gets spooked and runs right into a dinosaur. The dinosaur promptly picked the person up, chomped them in half, and the gory half of their body left hit the ground in front of us as we hid under a van. The dinosaur then picked it up and walked off.

Much of the demo was spent hidden in the brush observing and listening for the dinosaur that we had just seen eat someone. Every so often the loud thuds of it stomping in the forest could be heard, and most of the time we didn't see the dinosaur. Each stop was ominous and immediately upped the tension.

From the moment we saw the dinosaur, the feeling of the demo switched. Heart rate went up a bit, we were on alert, and all the classic markers of a horror game were present. In this case though, the big monster is a dinosaur we know can snap us up without much effort.

Developer Great Ape Games emphasized that this is just what dinosaurs would behave like, they're not trying to videogameify the dinosaurs in some way but just to naturally portray the fact that they're predators and you're prey.

While we didn't see them, you will eventually get some tools to "fight back." They didn't offer details, however.

All we really saw was the ability to pick up and throw the standard objects like glass bottles to make a sound and distract the dinosaur. Like, say, if you have a keycard and need to get it into a machine but need the dinosaur far enough away.

To up the more classic horror feel, you can only manually save if you have a save disk on you. They are found throughout the world and the machines to slot them in are scattered as well. They made sure to note that the fact you start putting in a disk isn't enough to save you, as there's a bit of a process and animation before the saving occurs. So, you'll need to be relatively safe, and it won't save you at the last second. Plan to die frequently.

As far as a demo presentation goes, The Lost Wild was great. The tension, the fear permeating from the screen, the overwhelming sounds… it accomplished its goal in making for a stressful experience.

With how common fodder dinosaurs are as enemies in games, it is refreshing to see them as truly terrifying as they actually would be if encountered in real life.

Survival horror fans should keep a close eye on The Lost Wild, especially if you're tired of the zombie aesthetic.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/lost-wild-may-be-dinosaur-horror-game-youre-looking-for
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Out of Words is A Beautiful Expression of Human Creativity and Art
item.content:encoded:
In a world where human creativity is genuinely under threat by AI, something like Out of Words existing as an undeniably human made passion project is amazing to see.

Out of Words is a co-op puzzle platformer that may be one of the most visually striking games ever made. That feels hyperbolic, but seeing the game in motion and just checking out almost any frame of a trailer, and you'll see what I mean.

Everything on the screen comes from a model or physical object crafted by human hands. The animations, facial expressions -- everything comes from the classic stop-motion tradition. Stop-motion films, even short films, in this style are a rarity and to see a game get this level of treatment is incredible.

If you look at the details on-screen close enough, you'll see what things are made of. The actual fabric the character's wear, the ribbons of thin colored plastic that make up floating seaweed underwater... the list is endless.

Talking to the development team, they talked about how in some ways their studio space is like any other: a bunch of PCs with unreal loaded up on them working on the game. But, in what seems like a more magical place, there's a huge space dedicated to crafting the models and world of Out of Words by hand, full of clays, fabrics, and all sorts of materials.

That may seem like a random aside, but it was a wonderful detail to hear when the more trailers and games that get released, the less certain we are of the generative AI usage in its development.

So, suffice to say, Out of Words is breathtakingly beautiful and shows some of the highest level of craft in the stop-motion medium, genuinely rivaling that of places like the legendary filmmaker Laika.

Out of Words takes place in a surreal world our heroes Kurt and Karla fall into because of their inability to talk with each other. They're deep friends who are moving onto a new chapter of their lives apart and neither of them wants to talk about the feelings they have for the other. That leads to their mouths disappearing and descending into this world.

The story is a very metaphorical one all about their relationship and running away from their worries and not having that frank conversation with one another. Huge monsters and black terrifying blobs are stand-ins for their anxiety, worries, and fears.

Throughout, there are a dozen or so gameplay mechanics in the journey. For our preview, we saw two. In one, it was your simple flipping of gravity, up was down sort of thing.

The other, also gravity related, saw us tossing back a little creature who, when he was with one character, would flip their gravity for them. So, it made for these trust fall moments where one of us would have to take a leap of faith and hope our partner tossed it over to swap the gravity and save ourselves.

In a particularly beautiful moment, we floated through air tossing the creature back and forth as we fell up and down in tandem.

Those are the only two gravity affecting mechanics, the rest throughout the game will be something different. They tested out hundreds of mechanics and settled on what's in the game due to how well they suited mirroring the relationship of Kurt and Karla. 

Out of Words Preview | Final Thoughts

The gameplay wasn't overly complicated, nor was it difficult, and it doesn't plan to be. Accessibility is a key pillar of the design, and developers Kong Orange and Wiredfly are hoping that Out of Words may be a great game to pick up with kids and non-gamers alike.

To some, those may be the words that turn them off from the game. But, from the brief snippet of the game and conversation I was able to have, the intention and metaphor behind the visuals and mechanics is what I find exciting. Blending it all in clever ways to further the story and give us insight into Kurt and Karla's relationship in a way only games can is sometimes even more exciting than the best combat systems out there.

Out of Words is for people that like the idea of a "vibes" game, where you're just there to soak up the experience. And, in this case, we'll get to soak in an experience with one of the most visually beautiful games out there.

Out of Words was previewed at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/out-of-words-is-beautiful-expression-of-human-creativity-and-art
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Control Resonant Sets You Loose as a Powerful Agent of Chaos
item.content:encoded:
When I saw that we were finally getting the sequel to Control, I never would have thought it would be a more open world hack and slash RPG, featuring combat in the vein of like Devil May Cry. I am so glad they made the choice with Control Resonant.

It's been seven years since Control (as an aside, I learned all Remedy games are set in the year they come out, who knew?), and just has much time has passed in the game world. Jesse has been director of the Federal Bureau of Control for a minute and… things aren't going well.

The Hiss have broken containment and overrun the bureau, eventually breaking out of The Old House altogether. Through means I'll let you learn yourself, Jesse's brother Dylan also gets released. Finally, we get to see the power everyone is so afraid of.

Control Resonant is the biggest departure from what is the usual more measured, slow-paced combat from Remedy's previous games. The arsenal they've given Dylan fully embraces that turn to fast-paced action.

The moment to moment gameplay is twitchy, combo-driven, and rewards fast reactions. Mid-combo dodges are a key to survival, and quickly zipping from one enemy to the next as a moving target makes for some beautiful controlled chaos.

Your arsenal consists of a light weapon, heavy weapon, combo finisher, and a slate of three abilities. All of these are customizable and change up the playstyle quite a bit.

The light attack is your "main" weapon in a sense, the one you'll be slashing with the most and those feel as you'd expect. They vary with effects, like one is lower damage with faster attacks and higher crit chance and another does more damage when hitting someone from behind.

One heavy attack is a massive hammer that smashes down, while another is a long bladed whip that lashes out at enemies and can hit in a big arc.

Combo finishers actually change quite a bit of the combat too. These are finishers that start up at the end of a light attack combo. One was a series of quick jabs that increased enemy stagger and another was this wide hard-hitting cleave.

Abilities were all over the place from a shield of earth, to slamming down on the ground causing a small shockwave, and even one where you shoot out a big burst of fire.

I bring all of that up to say, there is a lot you can do to customize Dylan's arsenal to play the way you want. And there's even a talent tree to vary the playstyles even more.

Another departure from the norm for Remedy, Resonant has large open spaces to explore. It's not exactly full on open-world, but there are large sandboxes to run around in and explore with a variety of enemies and events to partake in.

The world is a warped Manhattan that is stretched, flipped, endless, and, well, manipulated in anyway you can think of. Liminal spaces abound and the eerie floating bodies of the citizenry float everywhere.

I was able to explore a part of one of those open areas, and they are absolutely huge. There's a lot to see both horizontally and vertically, and Resonant gives you an incredible amount of tools to get just about anywhere you want.

Dylan can double jump, hold that jump to go up just a little higher and float for a bit to glide further, can dodge twice in the air to dash forward, and you can get a good sprint going before you go into a jump to get even further.

The distances you can travel are immense, with moments in the prologue that show you these jumps looking impossible. It's no wonder the FBC saw so much potential in Dylan.

Resonant gives you a level of freedom I would never have expected in a Control sequel. The original wasn't necessarily on-rails, but Resonant is really making this corrupted version of Manhattan your playground to fight the Hiss.

Control Resonant Preview | Final Thoughts

I greatly enjoyed my time with Control Resonant. The gameplay is focused, satisfying, and twitchy in the best ways. The world is just as weird as it was before, and I know I'll be in for a story with some awesome twists and turns.

Control Resonant releases in the very busy month of September, and I think this is one that should be towards the top of your lists.

Control Resonant was previewed at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/control-resonant-sets-you-loose-as-powerful-agent-of-chaos
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
